Table of Contents

Step-by-Step Instructions

5.1 Explore

Data Exploration to Understand Your Data

Before building any report, explore your dataset to validate what you have — data ranges, key metrics, and anomalies like zero-price records.

1. 5.1.1 Build a Multi-Row Card visual: Visualizations → Multi-row card → a blank visual appears on the canvas
2. 5.1.2 Drag Qty from the Data table into Fields → Sum of Qty (52,800.85) appears in the visual
5.1.3 Drag Price into Fields → Sum of Price appears → click the dropdown on it → select Average (Average of Price is far more meaningful than Sum of Price for analysis)
3. 5.1.3 (continued) Confirm the field now shows Average of Price and the aggregation change is reflected in the visual
4. 5.1.4 Continue enriching the KPI card — add these fields to the Multi-Row Card:
• Date (Earliest Date / Latest Date) — shows your reporting period (1 Jan 2023 – 31 Dec 2024)
• Count of Material — 5 distinct material types
• Count of Project — 3 projects
5. 5.1.5 Build a Matrix (Pivot Table): Visualizations → Matrix → add fields:
• Rows: Project, then Material
• Values: Sum of Qty, Average of Price
Then go to Visualizations → Format → General → Title → type "Summary by Project and Material"
6. 5.1.6 Create a 0-Price Matrix: Select the Summary matrix → Ctrl+C → Ctrl+V → rename title to "Summary of 0 Price" → drag Price to Filters on this visual → Basic Filtering → tick 0.00 only
7. 5.1.7 Create a Line Chart: Visualizations → Line Chart → build two trend charts:
• Sum of Qty by Year (X-axis: Date Year, Y-axis: Sum of Qty)
• Average of Price by Year (X-axis: Date Year, Y-axis: Average of Price)
These reveal how quantity and pricing move over time
💡Sum of Price across rows is misleading — individual unit prices are not cumulative. Always use Average of Price for meaningful comparisons across projects or time.
5.2 Plan

Planning and Creating a Report that Provides Insights

Jumping straight into visuals without a plan often results in cluttered, confusing reports. Good planning ensures your dashboard answers the right questions for the right audience.

1. 1. Define the Purpose
Ask: What business problem am I solving? What decisions should this enable? What questions will the audience ask?
Example: "I want to analyse material spending trends so procurement teams can identify cost-saving opportunities."

2. Identify the Audience
Executives → high-level KPI dashboard. Analysts → drill-down capability.

3. Select Key Metrics
Focus only on KPIs that matter: Qty, Price, Total Cost, Supplier Performance — aligned with business objectives.
2. 4. Plan the Layout and Flow
Think storyboard — overview → detail → drill-down:
• Page 1: Summary KPIs + trend lines
• Page 2: Breakdown by project / material
• Page 3: Data quality / exceptions

5. Consider User Questions
• Which projects have the highest spending?
• Are prices rising faster than quantities?
• Are there anomalies (zero-price records, unusual demand spikes)?
💡A good report is built backwards — start with the questions users will ask, then design the visuals that answer them.
5.3 Plan

Power BI Planning Example

Before building, review a concrete example of what each report page should contain — this is your blueprint for sections 5.4, 5.5, and 5.6.

1. Page 1 – Executive Overview
KPI Cards: Total Qty · Average Price · Total Cost · Count of Projects · Count of Materials · Qty with Zero Price (data quality indicator)
Trend Charts: Sum of Qty by Month/Year · Average of Price by Month/Year · (Optional) Combo chart with dual Y-axis
Questions answered: Is total spend rising? Is average price stable? Are there anomalies?
2. Page 2 – Project & Material Breakdown
Matrix: Rows = Project → Material | Values = Sum of Qty, Average of Price, Sum of Total Cost
Stacked Column Chart: X = Project | Values = Total Cost split by Material
Slicers: Date · Project · Material
Questions answered: Which projects spend most? Which materials dominate project cost?
3. Page 3 – Data Quality & Exceptions
Matrix: Rows = Project → Material | Values = Qty where Price = 0
Bar Chart: Qty with Zero Price by Project
Trend Line: Count of Zero Price records by Date
Questions answered: Which projects have zero-price records? Is the issue recurring? Does it bias our average price analysis?
💡Use this planning example as a checklist before you start building — confirm each page has a clear purpose, defined content, and at least one key question it answers.
5.4 Build

Create Executive Overview Page

The Executive Overview is the first thing leadership sees — it must deliver high-level KPIs and trend direction at a glance, without requiring any interaction.

1. 5.4.1 Create the Total Cost calculated column: Table View → Table Tools → New Column → enter formula: Total Cost = Data[Qty] * Data[Price] → press Enter
5.4.2 Create a new report page by clicking the + icon at the bottom
5.4.3 Right-click Page 2 → Rename → type "Executive Overview" → Enter
2. 5.4.4 Create a Multi-Row Card Visual: Visualizations → Multi-row card → add fields in order:
• Date Year
• Sum of Total Cost
3. 5.4.5 Create a Line Chart: Visualizations → Line Chart → configure:
• X-axis: Date Month | Y-axis: Sum of Total Cost | Legend: Date Year
Repeat this step for Sum of Qty and Average of Price to create three trend charts on this page
💡Total Cost = Qty × Price is the most important executive KPI. Creating it as a calculated column (not a measure) makes it reusable across all visuals and pages.
5.5 Build

Create Project & Material Breakdown Page

Operational teams need to drill into spending by project and material — this page enables that with a sortable matrix, column charts, and interactive slicers.

1. 5.5.1 Create a new page and rename it to "Project & Material Breakdown"
5.5.2 Create a Matrix Visual: Visualizations → Matrix → add fields:
• Rows: Project, then Material
• Columns: Date Year
• Values: Sum of Total Cost · Sum of Qty · Average of Price
2. 5.5.3 Turn off Column Subtotals: Select the Matrix → Visualizations → Format → Visual → Column subtotals → toggle OFF
5.5.4 Sort the Matrix: Hover over the visual → click the sort icon (top-right) → Sort by → Sum of Total Cost
5.5.5 Add a title: Format → General → Title → toggle ON → type "Project & Materials Breakdown" → Enter
3. 5.5.6 Create Clustered Column Charts: Visualizations → Clustered Column Chart
Chart 1: X-axis = Project | Y-axis = Sum of Total Cost | Legend = Date Year
Chart 2: X-axis = Material | Y-axis = Sum of Total Cost | Legend = Date Year
4. 5.5.7 Create Slicers: Visualizations → Slicer → drag in Date Year
Repeat to create slicers for Project and Material
These allow users to filter the entire page interactively
💡Always sort your matrix by the most important metric (Total Cost) so the highest-value items appear first — executives shouldn't have to hunt for the top performers.
5.6 Quality

Create Data Quality Control Page

Zero-price records can silently bias your Average Price calculations. This dedicated page surfaces those anomalies so they can be investigated or corrected before the report is shared.

1. 5.6.1 Duplicate the page: Right-click "Project & Material Breakdown" → Duplicate
5.6.2 Rename: Right-click the duplicate page → Rename → type "Data Quality Control" → Enter
5.6.3 Add a page-level filter: Expand the Filters pane → drag Price to Filters on this page
2. 5.6.4 Configure the filter: Set Filter Type → Basic Filtering → tick 0.00 only — now all visuals on this page show only zero-price records
5.6.5 Clean up the Matrix: Select it → remove Sum of Total Cost and Average of Price (keep only Qty for zero-price records)
5.6.6 Update the Column Charts: Replace the Y-axis in both charts from Sum of Total Cost to Sum of Qty — showing the volume of zero-price items by Project and Material
💡Zero-price records can distort Average Price downward. After identifying them here, decide whether to exclude them from your main analysis or flag them for data correction upstream.

Module 5 Recap

You have built a complete Power BI report from scratch — exploring data, planning report structure, and delivering three production-ready pages: Executive Overview, Project & Material Breakdown, and Data Quality Control.

The Data Quality Control page is especially powerful — zero-price records can silently distort your averages. By isolating them on a dedicated page, you ensure your executive KPIs are always accurate.

Combined with the clean data pipeline from Module 4, you now have an end-to-end Power BI solution — from raw Excel to executive dashboard.

🎉 All 6 sections completed! Your Power BI report is ready to share.
